era_context: The Egyptian 18th Dynasty is in its early phase of imperial consolidation. The Hittite Old Kingdom is expanding militarily. Mycenaean Greece is developing palace centers. The Shang Dynasty continues to dominate China. This period is characterized by the emergence of territorial empires based on chariot warfare and palace administration.
geographic_coverage_gaps: South Asia (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Continuation of Egyptian New Kingdom military consolidation
events.description: The Egyptian 18th Dynasty continues its program of military expansion and administrative consolidation. Nubian gold and resources flow northward to support the Egyptian state. Levantine vassal states pay tribute. The period is marked by the construction of temples and administrative infrastructure to support imperial rule. Specific events for this year are not well-documented in surviving sources.
key_figures: Ahmose I or his successor (exact reign boundaries uncertain)

sources.name: Temple reliefs and administrative records from the 18th Dynasty
Historiographic consensus on New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: This year falls within the reign of Ahmose I or possibly his immediate successor, but no specific events are documented for this particular year. The dating is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 1917 BCE in surviving sources. The assignment of this year to the reign of Ahmose I or his successor is based on conventional chronology, which is itself disputed. Some scholars would place this year in the reign of a different pharaoh entirely, depending on which chronological system is adopted.
historiographic_note: For this year, the historical record is essentially silent. No specific events are documented in Egyptian, Hittite, or other surviving sources. The era context is based on broader patterns of the period, but year-specific history is not available. This is typical for the Bronze Age; most years leave no documentary trace, and the assignment of events to specific years is often conventional or based on archaeological stratigraphy that cannot be dated more precisely than ±10–20 years.
